_meta.title: Contaminant → cheapest-effective household treatment mapping
_meta.note: Curated domain knowledge. Treatments are ordered cheapest-first among methods that are genuinely effective for the specific contaminant. Different contaminants need different treatments — this is not a generic 'buy an RO'.
sources: WHO Guidelines for Drinking-water Quality (4th ed.)

arsenic.note: Boiling does NOT remove arsenic and concentrates it. Use activated alumina or RO.

citation.reference: WHO GDWQ 4th ed., Arsenic — adsorption (activated alumina) and membrane (RO) are effective POU options

fluoride.note: Activated alumina is the standard low-cost defluoridation method used across India.

citation.reference: WHO GDWQ 4th ed., Fluoride — activated alumina / bone char / RO are recognised removal techniques

nitrate.note: Boiling increases nitrate concentration. Use ion-exchange or RO. Critical for infants (blue-baby syndrome).

citation.reference: WHO GDWQ 4th ed., Nitrate/Nitrite — ion exchange and RO are effective; boiling is counterproductive

lead.note: Use a filter certified for lead reduction (NSF/ANSI 53). Flushing taps also reduces first-draw lead.

citation.reference: WHO GDWQ 4th ed., Lead — RO, certified carbon-block, and distillation reduce dissolved lead

total_hardness.note: Hardness is an aesthetic/scaling issue, not an acute health risk; a softener is the targeted fix.

citation.reference: WHO GDWQ 4th ed., Hardness — ion-exchange softening is the standard treatment

total_coliform.note: Bacteria are the one contaminant boiling DOES fix cheaply. UV/UF/chlorination for continuous supply.

citation.reference: WHO GDWQ 4th ed., Microbial — boiling, UV, UF, and chlorination achieve microbial inactivation/removal

e_coli.note: E. coli indicates faecal contamination — treat as urgent. Boil immediately; install UV/UF for ongoing safety.

citation.reference: WHO GDWQ 4th ed., Microbial — thermal/UV/membrane/chemical disinfection are effective

tds.note: Only RO (or distillation) meaningfully lowers total dissolved solids.

citation.reference: WHO GDWQ 4th ed., TDS — membrane (RO) treatment for TDS reduction

iron.note: Iron is mostly aesthetic (taste/stains). Aeration-oxidation-filtration is the low-cost route.

citation.reference: WHO GDWQ 4th ed., Iron — oxidation and filtration; RO for dissolved iron

turbidity.note: Turbidity shields microbes from disinfection — clear it first, then disinfect.

citation.reference: WHO GDWQ 4th ed., Turbidity — filtration/coagulation before disinfection

_default.note: When several dissolved + microbial contaminants co-occur, an RO+UV unit is the broad-spectrum fallback.

citation.reference: WHO GDWQ 4th ed. — multi-barrier RO+UV for mixed contamination
